step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("complete transection of cranial part of accessory nerve effects")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the detailed review of the literature focusing narrowly back onto our original question - identifying which muscles remain active post-complete transection of the cranial part of the accessory nerve – we find strong support for option C, the stylopharyngeus muscle being notably spared. While other muscles serve essential functions in respiration and swallowing, they rely heavily on bilateral innervation from both the accessory nerve and others like the vagus. Specifically, the stylopharyngeus receives significant input from the glossopharyngeal nerve (IX), ensuring continued operation even amidst loss of the cranial accessory nerve components. Therefore, my stance aligns closely with prior conclusions emphasizing this unique characteristic of the stylopharyngeus.
